steps: The scatterplot shows data in an exponential pattern trending up from left to right — successive points accelerate upward, so the gaps between them grow.
A linear (straight-line) model cannot bend to follow accelerating data; eliminate any choice whose model is a straight line.
Choices A and B both use straight lines — A slants up but has 7 of 8 points below it (poor fit), B slants down (wrong direction entirely). Eliminate A and B.

Between the two exponential curves: Choice C has all 8 points below the curve (systematic overshoot — the curve is too high everywhere). Choice D has 2 points on the curve, 3 above, and 3 below — a balanced residual distribution, which is the hallmark of a good fit.
choiceAnalysis:
• Choice A — 'Line slanting up, 7 of 8 points below': A straight line cannot match exponential acceleration; 7 points below the line shows a systematic negative bias — the line fits poorly. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Line slanting down, 5 of 8 points below': The data trends up from left to right, so a downward-slanting line is in the wrong direction entirely. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Exponential curve, all 8 points below': An exponential curve is the right shape, but if every single point is below the curve the model overshoots everywhere — the curve is consistently too high and is not a best fit. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Exponential curve, 2 on, 3 above, 3 below': An exponential curve trending up matches the data shape, and having roughly equal points above and below indicates a balanced, unbiased fit. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
